title: Pañca-Parameṣṭhi Sajjhāy (Svādhyāya on the Five Supreme Beings)
summary: This is a short study-recitation (sajjhāy) that takes the five highest spiritual ideals of Jainism — here named one at a time — and declares each of them to be the reciter's blessing, deity, and refuge, while casting off one's own sin. It is recited daily in the closing study-portion of the Devasi (evening) pratikramaṇa. The pattern of every verse is identical, so once you learn one verse you can follow them all: only the name of the supreme being changes.
ritualPlacement: The sajjhāy belongs to the closing study-portion (svādhyāya) of the Devasi pratikramaṇa, not to the six Āvaśyakas. In the book's Devasi sequence it is the middle member of the three-fold sajjhāy cluster: Drumapuṣpikā Sajjhāy → **Pañca-Parameṣṭhi Sajjhāy** → Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ Sajjhāy, recited after Muni Vandanā and before the conclusion of the sāmāyika. As svādhyāya it is sacred study (one of the internal austerities), distinct from confession or veneration. Because Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī carry no independent text and Pakkhī uses the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys instead, this particular sajjhāy is proper to the Devasi study-cluster; do not transpose it elsewhere.
authorAndSource: — (no individual author is named in the book for this sajjhāy). It is a traditional maṅgala/svādhyāya verse-set on the Parameṣṭhins; the wording is fixed by the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition of Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit, which is the golden source for the readings given above.
structureArc: Four verses, one per supreme being, in the same fixed four-line frame ('X is my blessing; X is my deity; X — their glory is proclaimed; thus I cast off sin'). v.1 takes the **Arihantas** (the embodied omniscient victors who still teach); v.2 the **Siddhas** (the wholly liberated, bodiless souls); v.3 the **Ācāryas** (the heads/preceptors who lead the order); v.4 the **Upādhyāyas** (the teachers of scripture). The progression descends the Pañca-Parameṣṭhi hierarchy from the worshipped goal (Arihanta, Siddha) to the living guides (Ācārya, Upādhyāya). The fifth Parameṣṭhin, the Sādhu, is not given a separate verse here (see notes); the complete five are honoured together in the Navkar that opens the same ritual.
recurringImagery: Every verse repeats one threefold movement that you only need to understand once. (1) 'X maṅgalaṁ majjha' — the supreme being is my **maṅgala**, my auspicious blessing that removes obstacles. (2) 'X majjha devayā' — the supreme being is my **devatā**, the worthy object of my devotion (not a worldly deity who grants favours, but an ideal whose qualities I take refuge in). (3) 'X kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ' — 'their glory is proclaimed', a declaration of praise (kīrtana). The closing refrain 'vosirāmi tti pāvagaṁ' — 'thus I cast off (vyutsṛj) sin' — links the praise to release: by enthroning the Parameṣṭhins as blessing, refuge, and the praised ones, the reciter renounces evil/karmic stain. So each verse is a miniature movement from veneration → refuge → praise → abandonment of sin, applied successively to each of the four named ideals.
keyThemes: the Pañca-Parameṣṭhin as the supreme refuge
devotion (devatā) directed to spiritual ideals, not worldly gods
kīrtana — proclaiming the glory of the worthy
vyutsarga — casting off sin / renunciation of evil

glossary.definition: The five supreme beings worthy of worship: Arihanta, Siddha, Ācārya, Upādhyāya, and Sādhu — the five saluted in the Navkar.
An omniscient victor who has destroyed the inner enemies (passions) and, while still embodied, expounds the path; the first Parameṣṭhin (v.1).
A fully liberated, bodiless soul that has shed all karma and dwells in perfection at the summit of the universe; the second Parameṣṭhin (v.2).
The head and exemplar of the monastic order who upholds and transmits right conduct; the third Parameṣṭhin (v.3).
The preceptor who teaches the scriptures (śruta) to the order; the fourth Parameṣṭhin (v.4).
An ascetic striving on the path; the fifth Parameṣṭhin, honoured with the others in the Navkar though not given a separate verse here.
That which is auspicious and removes obstacles; here each Parameṣṭhin is declared to be the reciter's blessing.
Object of devotion/refuge; in this verse the Parameṣṭhins, not worldly gods, are taken as one's true 'deity'.
Proclamation of glory; 'kittiyaṁ tāṇaṁ' = 'their renown is proclaimed'.
Letting go / casting off; 'vosirāmi' = 'I abandon', here the renunciation of sin (pāpa).
Sin, demerit, karmic stain — what the reciter casts off in the closing refrain.
Sacred study-recitation, an internal austerity; the closing study-portion of the pratikramaṇa, distinct from the six Āvaśyakas.

howToRead: Newcomers: read just one verse closely — 'this supreme being is my blessing, my refuge, the one I praise; so I let go of my sin' — then notice that the other three verses say exactly the same thing about the next ideal, so the whole sajjhāy is one prayer repeated through four names. Advanced practitioners: weigh the deliberate descent through the Parameṣṭhi hierarchy (Arihanta → Siddha → Ācārya → Upādhyāya) and the binding of kīrtana to vyutsarga in the refrain — devotion enacted as the active shedding of pāpa.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim verse readings, the Devasi sajjhāy cluster placement, and orthography (golden source).

sources.title: Namaskāra (Navkāra) Mantra and the Pañca-Parameṣṭhin — overview
sources.consultedFor: Identity and standard ordering of the five supreme beings (Arihanta, Siddha, Ācārya, Upādhyāya, Sādhu).

notes: The sutra is titled 'Pañca (Five) Parameṣṭhi Sajjhāy', yet the verses supplied name only four Parameṣṭhins (Arihanta, Siddha, Ācārya/āyariya, Upādhyāya/uvajjhāya); the fifth, the Sādhu, has no separate verse in the text given here. This may reflect that all five are jointly honoured in the Navkar that opens the same ritual, or that a Sādhu verse exists in some recensions but is not among the four printed in this edition — flagged as uncertain; the book's printed text is followed as authoritative and not emended. The morphology of the Prakrit (e.g. 'mujjha' = Skt. 'mama/mahyam' = my/to me; 'tāṇaṁ' = 'teṣāṁ' = of them; 'tti' = quotative 'iti') is given to aid reading and is not a variant reading. Per the book's orthography rules, spellings such as 'મુજઝ' and the period before the refrain are preserved verbatim and not normalized.
